note: Concise source-definition notes from local textbook PDFs. These notes support the CFA-inspired classroom definitions; they do not replace the course wording.

sources.title: Essentials of Investments, 12e ISE
sources.authors: Zvi Bodie, Alex Kane, Alan J. Marcus
sources.file: Essentials of Investments, 12e ISE (Zvi Bodie Alex Kane Alan J. Marcus).pdf

sources.file: Investment Analysis and Portfolio Management (Frank K. Reilly,Keith C. Brown etc.).pdf

sources.title: The Little Book of Valuation

sources.title: Why Does the Stock Market Go Up?
sources.file: Why Does the Stock Market Go Up Everything You Should Have Been Taught About Investing In School, But Werent (Brian Feroldi).pdf

matches.definition: A current commitment of money or other resources made with the expectation of future benefits.
Taking risk because the expected risk-return trade-off is favourable, unlike gambling for risk itself.
A financial security representing ownership of a fraction of a corporation.
A place where businesses and investors connect to buy, sell and issue shares of publicly held companies.
The speed and ease with which investors can realize the cash value of an investment.
The price at which a dealer or market participant is willing to buy an asset.
The price at which an investor would need to buy from the market; paired with the bid in the bid-ask spread.
The return earned over the period during which the investment is owned.
A gain that occurs when an asset is sold for a higher price than its purchase price.
A payment of part of a company's profits to its shareholders.
A percentage ratio estimating the cash return from a stock or fund, calculated as dividend per share divided by current share price.
The current dollar value of a company's equity, found by multiplying total shares by the current market price of one share.
A basic valuation ratio that divides price per share by earnings per share.
Intrinsic value depends on the cash flows an asset is expected to generate and the uncertainty of those cash flows.
Relative valuation estimates value by comparing how the market prices similar assets.
Gross profit divided by sales.
Operating profit divided by sales.
Operating profit is described as EBIT, or earnings before interest and taxes.

matches.sourceTerm: free cash flow to the firm
matches.definition: After-tax operating cash flow net of investments in fixed capital and working capital, available to debt and equity holders.
Cash flow available to stockholders after payments to other capital suppliers and after funding continued growth.
A collection of financial assets that can be bought or sold on an exchange like a stock.
An offshoot of mutual funds that lets investors trade index portfolios as they do shares of stock.
A portfolio of stocks or bonds designed to match the composition and performance of a financial market index.
Holding many assets so exposure to any particular asset is limited.
A risk management strategy that mixes financial assets in a portfolio to reduce risk and improve long-run return potential.
Risk from influences that affect one firm without noticeably affecting other firms; diversification can reduce it.
The rate at which domestic currency can be converted into foreign currency.
Recurring patterns of economic expansion and contraction, with peaks and troughs across the cycle.
Industries with above-average sensitivity to the state of the economy, often benefiting early in recovery.
The phase after a trough when the economy enters expansion again.
The split between fixed and variable costs; high fixed costs make profit more sensitive to sales changes.
Industry competition can be analysed through entry threats, rivalry, substitutes, buyer power and supplier power.
Portfolio return is a function of component security returns and portfolio weights.
Brand names, customer loyalty and a trained workforce are examples of intangible assets that can carry value.
